item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has been my favorite SCP for years. It’s so deeply unsettling and equally intriguing.

My interpretation:

+ show block
– hide block

I think Olivia might not literally be their daughter. I think she might be the knot in reality that clearly exists inescapably intertwined with this family manifesting itself as a person. Specifically, I think she might be the personification of her 'parent's' screwed up relationship that they can no longer address. It seems that, according to them, they have her, and then three other much younger kids who are close in age. That's an odd pattern in which to have children. I'm thinking it's because 'Olivia' has existed since the start of their relationship, because she is a part of their relationship, and she takes the form of their child because she's something they created together. That's why they can't move, can't leave, can't get away from the house. Because the anomaly isn't really in the attic. It's in them.

Or, well, that's a clear, logical explanation. But I don't think this SCP really has a clear, logical explanation behind it. I used to have these fucked up dreams where my family's late rabbit is alive again and we haven't been taking care of her for so long and I feel like we failed her and I don't know when or how she's going to leave again, and because of that I feel like I know EXACTLY what this SCP is, but not in a way that means I can explain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've had dreams like this, where I stand up and open the bedroom door only to end up back in bed again - and again and again.

item.title: Re: Attribution concerns
item.content:encoded: The image was taken by the author, djkaktus, and is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: Attribution concerns
item.content:encoded: Posting as reply to author post due to relevance. No image attributions seem to be present. Could someone have this fixed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Today, something about this really struck me. I doubt it was part of kaktus's original intent, but it's a testament to an author's skill the more ways people can read into their work.

Ugly family secrets that no one ever talks about are nasty spectres. The things that go on that, when you can talk about them, you don't want to, and when you want to talk about them, you just can't. They hang over everybody involved, and everyone knows that everyone else knows, but it's not something you discuss when it's not actively happening.

You can ignore it early on, as happenstance occurances, but once it becomes a pattern you have to learn to live with it. And by the time you actually can talk about it, it's set in so thoroughly that talking won't get rid of it.

You wonder what might be a solution. You think about actually acknowledging it with some kind of intervention, but no one is willing to do so. You think about leaving, but would that even work or would it follow you? Can you leave something that's ultimately a product of your own mind?

No matter what you consider doing about it, you know you'll never go through with it, because you've already got the notion that it will never go away so engrained in your head

But the more it festers, the more it draws out the negativity in you. The fear, anger, loathing, and everything else that leaves you wondering if this is leading to some kind of conclusion. Maybe you or someone else in the house will finally have had enough and put a bullet in everyone's head one night after bedtime. Sometimes you wish that would happen too. Because until it does, you're just left with the lingering thought that it's what will happen.

That's what this article means to me. The worst kind of familial bond, over the awful things that leave you feeling like you're family is condemned to suffer silently together because of them. No hope to free yourself from them except maybe a murder-murder-murder-suicide that you sometimes fantasize about in your angriest moments, because you're all guilty and you all deserve to die because you're all tainted by the secret.

Maybe I'm just projecting my own baggage, but that's what this skip invokes in my mind

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: amazing skip with one of the most memorable lines in the wiki, but the banner contradicts itself by saying both 2/2740 classified and 3/2740 classified

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice! I like how it is good except that it never existed, which would make it good if it was real! Chilling and subtle, while being very clear about what precisely the skip is about. Not a fan of the continuation randomly being on a different page, though. +1

+ Nice.
- File name is scp-2740420.png.
- File name is scp-2740420.png.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty creepy. A house that hates you and wants you to be trapped and afraid, and does so by gaslighting you. Or maybe it's a stillborn corpse in a box, denied a proper burial and exacting its posthumous revenge… well OK, that one maybe belongs more in a modern jumpscare shockporn horror film…

Funny thing is, some of these perceptual effects are very much like the description of moderate (subfatal but certainly psychoeffective) carbon monoxide poisoning. Maybe if it's something you personally felt/experienced in the past, that was the source?

Also, how exactly did this come to foundation attention, and stick around long enough to get a classification? Someone experimenting with some heavy Mnestics and anti-antimemetics had a sudden rush of clarity?
(and seconding the "if we can't get in there even by dynamiting the house, how did we get the photo?" question…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, this is a good one, if only for the way the symbolism all fits together, and for the way it really articulates a nuanced emotional state. It's that psychological state you can find yourself in where you keep thinking about doing something but never actually do it because you dread something about it. You do that enough, and it becomes a bad habit that you find yourself trapped in. It also reminds me of those dreams I've had where I dream that I'm getting up and getting ready in the morning, only to wake up and realize that I'm still in bed. The thought of that happening in your waking life is terrifying.

I have to commend the writing for deeply disturbing me without the use of a spooky monster or blood and gore. Situational horror is what gets to me the most. The context and the feelings of confusion, helplessness, and dread are what make horror, not set-pieces.

Mechanically, this SCP is pretty confusing. I mean, how can they know they ran certain tests that they have no record of? Where those just tests the researchers remembered doing, but never actually did? If so, WHERE THE HOLY HELL DID THAT IMAGE COME FROM?

The mystery of what layers of this SCP are real and which aren't, is probably the most chilling aspect of the whole thing. As you read it, you start to wonder if you can trust any of it. Nothing can be tested because every time someone sets out to do something, it turns out it was all in their head. We don't know what facts can even be verified. For all we know, this is an abandoned house, all these people are either no longer alive or never existed, and these transcripts come from the hallucinations of the researchers who never visited the site in the first place. Some of the people probably exist, but we can't even tell if the person the whole thing pivots around ever existed at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those articles that I don't really understand, (maybe that's the point?) but I love it so much more for that. The sense of dread that builds at the start is fantastic.

The way the story is told here reminds me a lot of Lovecraft's The Color Out of Space, mostly because there is a scene involving attics as well as indescribable horror, but also because like the alien color, this thing can't be described.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my personal favorite of djk's articles. The core concept is solid, the exposition is done really well, and there's a perfect balance of revelation and mystery.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Belated suggestion; move the link to the tale near the end, as that tale is basically iterating on the plot twist of the end of this article. Linking it early on would tip that hand too early. Probably by the very end of the last interview log. Maybe linking it in:

Currently, it is believed that Olivia Lee does not exist, nor has ever existed.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: Noted - will reflect in upcoming edit.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The tale Leeway is about this article; it might be a good idea to link to it by changing this:

Franklin Lee: Our oldest daughter, Olivia.

to this:

Franklin Lee: Our oldest daughter, Olivia.

Just a suggestion.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Proposed Edits
- Proposed Edits

The edit proposed is to link the article to the Site-81 dossier, as the article mentions the site as part of its description, the geographical location of the article and the site are in close proximity, and that the author of this article is also the author for the Site-81 dossier.

Editing this:

Following this interview, Mr. Lee and his family were moved to Site-81 for holding and examination, with release pending further study of their mental condition

To this:

Following this interview, Mr. Lee and his family were moved to Site-81 for holding and examination, with release pending further study of their mental condition

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this one would make for a great parody in the J-section

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For future visitors, what the titles were (without having to scroll through 200 pages of edit history):

Current title: It Wasn't There

item.title: My Theory
item.content:encoded:
I know this is a bit old but i just read it recently. I think that the thing in the attic is Olivia's corpse. I think her parents killed her.

The anomaly gives some physical form to their emotions and thoughts. the 'hate building up' comment. I think the reason they can't leave is that they are trapped, in a very literal and physical sense, by their guilt of that they did.

They are also in complete denial of what they did too. This denial prevents anyone from checking what is in the attic because proving what it is would break the denial. The denial has also created a faux Olivia elsewhere. Her parents convinced themselves that they didn't kill her, they just had a bad fight 'the knife incident' and she left, so that was made partially real too, but not fully. THAT Olivia is just a fantasy and not real so the foundation wasn't able to confirm her after extra investigation.

This goes with the story that the mom actually DID go up there once, but is in denial about the details. The stand out comment form that was when pressed she said 'it wasn't Olivia'. Why the hell would she say that? no one THOUGHT that IT was Olivia, she just said that. Well that was the clue, its NOT Olivia, its her body. The mom went up to hide it in the attic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coming to this long after the fact, I just wanna note that the picture at the beginning REALLY sells it for me. It just really adds to the whole "is there something there, or is it just empty?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oddly enough SCP-2470 and SCP-2740 go really well together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very eerie and creepy. This stayed inside my head for a long time after reading it - that's gotta be a good sign right? At least for this article.
Upvoted, and upvoted again (apparently no evidence of second upvote exists)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy hell that was great.

This is easily one of the most dread-inducing articles on the site, there's just so many unsettling undertones about it all (not the least of which are the reasons for Olivia's departure from the house - provided she even exists). And that photo.

What I find most remarkable about this, though, is how easily the article could have failed - there's literally nothing concrete here, we aren't told anything that's even remotely reliable. Yet somehow this manages to exist in that weird, perfect sweet spot where what's left out only adds to the greatness of the article.

Terrific job, I think this is one of my new favorites.

+1 (hey, look, I made it turn to +200!)

Also, this line creeped me out like you wouldn't believe:

Yvette Lee: …I don't know. It wasn't Olivia.

O_O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the many things in life that end up fucking with my head so much I get close to anxiety attacks.

I love it to death, but this is really creepy in a realistic kind of way. Being aware of r e a l i t y being cut up and broken around you is one thing. But inevitability of the situation and the breaking point where she realizes it's something they accidently put in the house via their conflicts, or something to that effect…

It's fucking 12 in the morning, take your +1 and I thought I +1ed this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice one!
It realy displays the feel of not being able to trust the own mind.
Maybe they have a wild instance of SCP-055 in their Attic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to thank you for this, kaktus, as it's one of the reasons I wanted to become a member of this site.

This is a fantastic SCP. Thank goodness the whole thing about scaring people into comas and reality levels were removed. There's no need to tell anyone that this thing is scary. It already is scary.

And that's one of my favorite things about this article. The SCP says, "SCP-2740 appears to instill a sense of intense dread in any individual cognizant of it, although the reason for this is unknown." To me, the reason everyone dreads this thing isn't because it makes people dread it through anomalous force-people-to-feel-fear effect; they dread it for the same reason we, the readers, dread it: because it undermines any certainty as to what, if anything, is real.

It's original, it's well-executed, the surprise ending is great, and it's one I find myself going back to read every now and again because it's just that good. A pleasure to upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. It had been a while since I read any SCP that could actually unnerve me. Also, as a part of a house that is creepy and not entirely fathomable, it reminded me somewhat of House of Leaves, which is a big plus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the suggestion of LurkD, you all should read that cool tale I wrote about this. It's kaktus-approved, even.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This whole thing is so unnerving to me. It's shit like this that makes me hesitate to walk around the house at night. Your concept is so strong that it strums all the right notes in people's psyche. I don't know if you had that in mind or not, but the fear of what is real, what is not real, and what is unknown is a powerful thing. This works on so many levels for me even without the context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was confusing as fuck. But I liked it +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I'm reminded of those (though I'd forgotten about 303 until I read your comment), crossed with the Twilight Zone episode "And When the Sky Was Opened".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Taking Olivia's final line as the starting point, it makes sense to see 2740 as a manifestation of pure hate. To elaborate:

  • It hates reality (because it is the ultimate thing to hate).
    • It would choose to not be real if it could, but apparently it can't, so it's stuck in a state of being partially real.
    • It hates having its reality confirmed by people knowing about it. But it seems that it has a limitation of being unable to wipe people's knowledge of it, so as a substitute, they are punished by dread.
    • It hates having its reality confirmed via observation, so it undoes any attempt to observe it. Again, though, it can't wipe memories, so those remain intact.
      • Except, perhaps, when it was observed by Yvette. But that's a last-ditch measure. If it's not the case, then I don't have a good explanation.
  • It hates when Olivia left. Maybe Olivia somehow created it — she left her hate behind when she left, somehow? That would be corroborated by the interviews with her and her mother.
    • It can't bring Olivia back, somehow, or maybe it's disavowed her. As substitutes, it takes her family prisoner.
    • By bringing Olivia to talk about it, the Foundation prompted 2740 to finally unexist her, lest she provide more insight.

It's also possible that 2740 is something much, much more powerful and very blatant in its manifestation, but only in a different universe. Its reflection through this universe's iteration of the Lee family is "blurry", reality-wise, so it's sort-of-there sort-of-not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuckin creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ discussion of what is in Mikealike's spoiler so this is also spoilered
- NO Y U CLICK ON SPOILER?

That is one thing that occurred to me, and I would certainly consider it to be a possibility.
Specifically, this put the idea in my head: "My husband and I, we've been good parents, but we fought with Olivia over so many things, things that seem inconsequential now, but… it drove a wedge between us, and we all knew there was something festering in that space."

"It" is such a wonderfully vague word, as are "us," "something," and "that space." Did "it" (the constant arguments) drive a wedge between "us" (parents and child) or did "it" (the accidental death/murder of their daughter) drive a wedge between "us" (husband and wife)?

"Something" (animosity/corpse) was festering in "that space" (hole in family bond/attic).

"We all knew," so the other children knew of arguments that the parents and older child tried to hide from them, or the parents told them that the dead daughter moved in with the aunt, but the children knew that she was dead in the attic (and could probably smell the festering corpse). Maybe the aunt is a relative who lives far away who the children have never met and is actually deceased, and so "moving in with the aunt" turns into a euphemism for "joining the aunt in death."

So that turned into an extended analysis without my intending to do so. Can you tell I have a degree in English?

I have no idea if kaktus intended for *any* of that to be read into it, but even if he didn't, I love the ambiguity and the possibilities it allows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday, upon the stair,
I met a man who wasn't there.
He wasn't there again today,
I wish, I wish he'd go away…

EDIT: Also, this has shades of Gone Home (the game), what with the parent/daughter arguments, daughter leaving home, and the attic being a focal point for their troubled parent/child relationship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 And spoilers plz so I can sleep again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it at all but it's so delightfulły esoteric that I'm definitely feeling a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it seems that this complaint has been resolved, which I consider a shame, because [ACCESS DENIED] is an excellent title for this, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just incredible, Kaktus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Joined the site properly because of the creep level of this story and felt it needed more +1

item.title: ~~Updated~~
item.content:encoded: Made some edits based on feedbacks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very good example of format screw employed correctly. The strange nature of how the information is recorded and presented to the reader is both warranted thematically by the phenomenon in question, and serves a narrative purpose. The conflicting nature of what's being presented further underscores the uncanny, and frankly dreadful feeling instilled by whatever is going on in the attic.

There are various kinds of horror in writing, and when people say that we've moved away from horror, I think one of the things we mean is that the site has moved largely away from what I would term the "immediate terror" sub-genre of horror. Many earlier articles were about scary monsters who would come and fuck your shit up if they ever broke out of their Supermax Monster Jail, and by god some of those worked, and continue to work. But we've definitely moved on from that; you can only do it so many times. This, on the other hand, is of the creeping anxiety variety of horror. The unsettling, which can grow, god willing, into full-fledged existential terror in some cases. And I think as we all continue to refine the format in Series III, we can keep pushing this kind of writing as far as we care to. Horror lives on, it's just moved into the walls now.

I do have to throw in with the above commenters on the "terror to death" element. The feeling I got from the article as a whole was too pleasing to hold my upvote hostage over it, but I very strongly feel that you could strike the entire last paragraph of the description, and the article would be much better for it.

Overall, though, excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Currently, it is believed that minor distortions in local reality may be the cause of these dissonances, although current technology has been unable to verify this.

This line adds absolutely nothing to the article. It doesn't convey any information of interest, nor set up for something later on. Just cheapens everything by proffering an explanation for the weirdness that doesn't, in actuality, explain anything, even if it were the case.

The bit about dying of fear-induced brain damage, or whatever that is, doesn't make any sense, and it's not actually necessary for this to work. Feels like tacked-on danger and this doesn't need it. Some other effect, maybe, or maybe elaborate on it a bit more, make it weirder and less straight-up woke up dead.

The Lee family has not been moved to Site-81. Information claiming otherwise has been proven inaccurate. Research into this is ongoing.

Claiming to stating, subtract been, and remove "Research into this is ongoing." I feel like the research line tries too hard to build this up as mysterious, and it's not needed. Might just be me.

I don't like the bit about the company disappearing. I feel like there's more subtle ways Franklin could get gaslighted on that which would carry more impact, such as, he makes arrangements, then finds himself with the number dialed and the telephone in his hand, but hasn't hit "talk" yet.

several attempts were made to penetrate the attic, both manned and unmanned. In all cases, further investigation revealed that no attempts were actually made

This could be worded better. "… several plans were drafted to penetrate the attic…" and then say, "However, records indicate no attempts were actually made…" Start next paragraph off, "These plans included…"

You're saying that these attempts were and were not made. I get the idea, here, but this dissonance, of stating attempts were and were not made, doesn't make sense in the article, because you've got the article's author ascribing equal weight to memories and extant records, right up until they're not.

Feldman's closing lines bother me. They're cryptic statements apropos of nothing.

All in all, this feels like 3/4 of something good. It just doesn't feel complete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God damnit. This SCP was really good, but a stupid title like that really detracts from the classic feel that I loved so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought I had upvoted this already, apparently I didn't. +1

item.title: (no title)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …aaand +1 for giving me yet another reason to be freaked out by my attic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: noooooo i am super sekrit 1337 h4ck3rrr

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kaktus, get off of Researcher Dios's account. :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Hi I'm djkaktus and all I do is write good scps all day erryday. Look at me!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Niiiiicccce. this is a well done scp, so much to think about, its spooky, and makes you feel all hmmm inside.

+ Maybe spoiler
- Most likely not spoilers, just me making theory's 

Is the girl dead in the attic and every one is just refusing to enter it knowing whats up there but not really? and the reason no one else can go up there is that their feelings of "Cant go up there there's nothing up there" making it anomalous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I was confused as hell about this until I read the comments, but honestly, I liked it already and that just made it better. And frankly, I should have gotten it at the picture. Also, you can add me to the list of people who hate Humes, but I think they were pulled off tastefully and subtly here.

However!

This dread coalesces into full terror

kaktus plz

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Every basement I have ever been afraid to enter, every unexplained sense of unease I have ever gotten from a room or a place, it all came hurtling back. The title was a nice touch.

I regret I have but one up vote to give for this scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ..it isn't homeopathy in-universe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is brilliant. This is so good I was late visiting friends because I refused to leave the house because I was in the middle of this article. And the title seals it for me. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was wondering how this was all going to be explained, until:

Spoilers!
– hide block

There's only so much that hate can build up in a place before it starts hating you back…

That's both evocative and concise. +1'd for that.

I have to agree that the "full terror" line sounds awfully unscientific. Not enough to deny an upvote over, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's wrong with Humes? The technology isn't all that out-there. I mean, it's extremely over-simplified but there's nothing in real-world science that says you couldn't create a baseline "reality structure" reading.

Let's look at what we know about the Universe. Time is (again, simplifying) the fastest speed of information. What's the fastest way to get information? Light. Therefore, lightspeed is the speed of time. So, we have defined what Time is. From our perspective it's the one-way travel of information.

What can affect time(actually, light)? Gravity. Speed. What, therefore, would be a disruption of reality? A black hole. As far as we know, a black hole breaks a pretty serious law, the law that information cannot be destroyed. If this law is indeed a constant, then a black hole is a breakdown of the structure of reality, that is (apparently, thankfully) self-contained. It can't expand because it's pulling at itself just as strong as it's pulling at everything else.

Using this as a base, we can therefore make assumptions on what else might affect local reality. Like, say, super-high distortions of gravity that do not exist long enough to affect anything but the largest fundamental forces. It doesn't rip us apart(normally) because it's only around long enough to exert an influence on light(time) and what have you.

How would you read this? Well, if we're assuming this is working using super-powerful gravity, you just get a super-sensitive measure and tell it to record *every* change no matter how minute, and to alert you if it goes beyond what you'd consider the normal fluctuation of gravity on earth(which is a thing).

There are other ways you could probably bend reality over a table and fuck it, probably through super-strong magnetic forces or the strong nuclear-forces, but gravity is the one we know for certain that works, and it's one we can reliably test and measure.

Now, this is all pretty theoretical, but it is based in hard science. I'm making assumptions on the nature of black holes and how time actually works, but that's only because that's all we have right now. I also probably have numerous errors in this, which someone will hopefully point out.
